item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was a correct presumption! Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting that I reverted an edit by user Alton Blake that introduced additional content, presumably without the author's consent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was somewhat annoyed by the note in the Event 04 log- convenient little explanatory notes provided with SCPs for no reason tend to feel pretty arbitrary and half-assed, as if the author couldn't figure out a better way to explain what's going on. And this article does an excellent job of showing us what's going on, so the note felt doubly unnecessary here.

+ Then I read your comment.
- Then I read your comment.

Now I get why the first note was necessary- to provide a contrast to the second, creating a comparison which tells us just enough about how the notes are made to be creepy without being overly informative.

I still think you could pull it off without the first six lines of the first note, though. All you need for the comparison is one use of "prince" in reference to SCP-2181. We can already tell he's running and being chased, and we don't need to know why (not that the note tells us much about that).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not that often an SCP wants to be locked up, and is glad of the Foundation's protection. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, leaving aside the well-known … —- …, there are online Morse Code translators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well-played. Well-played, indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had enough Morse to pass my exam and that was about it. I barely remember more than a few letters, these days. If you don't take it out and use it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"print" is an intentional misspelling, for the record.

Spoilery explanation
-.-.

If you look at a previous version, you'll see it was originally "princ". But then I realized c in Morse code is -.-., and if you have the time to carve that into a human arm, you have enough time to carve another . for the e. So I decided to cut it off halfway (no pun intended), because -. is t. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
di-dit
dah-di-dit dah-dah-dah.

To answer a little less snarkily: ham radio operators who have had their licenses more than a decade tend to know it, even if they are a bit rusty. I'm about a 5 WPM code reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, am I alone in wondering if the 'print' in the addendum is a misspelling of 'prince' because who the hell knows Morse code these days?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also had the thought that it was a bit convenient that it showed up there too, but then I read that line about it coming to the foundation on purpose. One gets the sense from various skips that the foundation is pretty well known in the eldritch gossip networks as a decent last-resort refuge should you manage to piss off something out of your league. Its effectively a seven year old mentally, so it could have been trying to end up in the lobby and just happened to pick the wrong door. Or maybe that was the only one connected to its network at the time… who's to say how these things work?

Also, the single, building, single men's bathroom per floor thing makes sense to me: There are sites that are stated to be only a single building (I remember one of the canon-ish ones is a converted apartment building in New York), and my college dorm only had one bathroom per floor (around 25-30 rooms per floor, 2 people each). It was just a really big one in a central area, right next to the elevators and stairs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am not sure I like the teleporting to the restroom origin. It seems silly and smacks of the "discovered while looking in closets at Site-Redacted" cliche). Also that site only has one building and that building only has one restroom per floor?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't really find a concise way of explaining what happened to the door, so I'm just leaving it. The detail is meant to add some atmosphere, but I think if I add any more, it'll slow down the log. EDIT: I added an image instead! Thank you for pointing out the dry eyes thing, however, I found a name for it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've tightened a couple things, changed a couple things, and put in one major change that should hopefully make this better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and SCP-2181 suffers from dry eyes.

This doesn't seem like a clinical term, so iffy on the tone of this chunk.

Personnel mobilized outside containment cell report doorway is obscured by view of deep space;

What do you mean by deep space? Is there just a bit of vacuum sitting in the middle of the doorway? Or is it a portal to a random bit of deep space? Or is there some picture just waving around in front of them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, it walked out of the door when it hadn't been in the lavatory in the first place. Anomalies! As for your second question, "SCP-2181 has not aged visibly while in containment"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not, the author or staff, just strolling through.

I might be wrong, but I don't think that "introverted" is really a term that would be used in a scientific context. I think this could be left out and you'd be better off for it.

As far as I'm aware, introverted is a valid term, and makes sense in this context.

Binary like ASCII? As interpreted by computers, binary isn't really compatible with those occult elements (it's a really specific and clumsy way to do code for anything but computer use). Morse code would probably work, and I think it'd fit better in the theme.

Why would Morse be better? After all, both are just encoded languages utilizing a set of two values. (1/0 and dot/dash respectively). And if we're dealing with some kind of alien or extra-dimensional, who is more advanced than us, it actually makes sense in my mind that they would use one of the most advanced forms of communication we have, computer code.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its behavior when left alone is considered normal for an introverted seven year old child.

I might be wrong, but I don't think that "introverted" is really a term that would be used in a scientific context. I think this could be left out and you'd be better off for it.

Bones were inscribed with a series of lines that, when transcribed as binary code, translate as follows:

Binary like ASCII? As interpreted by computers, binary isn't really compatible with those occult elements (it's a really specific and clumsy way to do code for anything but computer use). Morse code would probably work, and I think it'd fit better in the theme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2181 was contained on March 1, 1976, when it exited Site-81's third floor men's lavatory

Shouldn't that be "entered", not exited? Also, maybe comment on how its appearance has or hasn't changed since recovery?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote were it not for the bad scare quotes in the addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it. I honestly don't know why it's currently in the negatives.

This raises questions in a good way, and raises them without shoving the reader's nose in them. Overall, this is pretty damn good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get the distinct impression I'm missing something.

On the surface, it's a cogent, fairly predictable alien/interdimensional runaway story. I read all the way through waiting for an "a-ha!" turn or revelation, but if it's there, it passed me by.

Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I get what you're going for now. Might note something about this rendering the iris unable to constrict fully or something. That would also make this guy extremely light sensitive, as well, like having your eyes permanently dilated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not the same overall size, just the same width. So it'll be disproportionately thin, in comparison.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite the pupil being six times larger than normal, the irises are of standard width.

I'm confused as to how that works. (The pupil being the hole encompassed by the muscle ring of the iris, you can't increase the diameter of one without increasing the diameter of the other.)

Otherwise this is weird enough to catch my interest and my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine my surprise when I found this little guy more or less complete on my hard drive! Thanks to SoundChaser, themanofum, and Decibelle for feedback. (Also, "a way" is intentional, please no one change it.)

Image credits: Taken from this page, licensed under Creative Commons.
